questions.q: What did Karna give away to the disguised god who came begging?
questions.explanation: Karna gave away the golden armor and earrings he had been born with, his greatest protection in battle.

questions.q: Which god came to Karna disguised as a beggar to ask for his armor?
questions.explanation: Indra, king of the gods and father of Arjuna, came in disguise to strip Karna of his natural protection.

questions.q: In what form did Indra approach Karna for the gift?
questions.explanation: Indra came disguised as a brahmin seeking alms, knowing Karna never refused a brahmin's request.

questions.q: Who warned Karna in advance of Indra's plan to beg away his armor?
questions.explanation: Surya, the sun god and Karna's true father, warned his son that Indra would come begging for the armor.

questions.q: How did the warning about Indra's scheme reach Karna?
questions.explanation: Surya appeared to Karna in a dream at night to reveal Indra's plan and counsel him against giving.

questions.q: How were Karna's armor and earrings attached to him?
options: Fused to his body from birth
Locked on by a divine mantra
questions.explanation: Karna was born with the kavacha and kundala grown into his very flesh, gifts of his divine father Surya.

questions.q: How did Karna part with the armor and earrings?
options: He melted them in sacred fire
He had a smith remove them
He cut them from his living flesh
questions.explanation: Since the armor was part of his body, Karna sliced it away from his bleeding flesh and handed it to the beggar.

questions.q: What did Karna receive from Indra in exchange for the armor?
options: The Vasavi Shakti, a divine dart
A shield no blade could pierce
questions.explanation: Indra gave Karna the Vasavi Shakti, an infallible dart that could be used only a single time.

questions.q: How many times could the dart given by Indra be used?
questions.explanation: The Vasavi Shakti would slay one enemy and then return to Indra, never to serve Karna again.

questions.q: Why did Indra want to strip Karna of his armor?
questions.explanation: Indra, father of Arjuna, knew Karna could not be slain while he wore the armor, and acted to safeguard his son before the war.

questions.q: What did Surya advise Karna to do about Indra's coming visit?
options: To hide until the god departed
To offer gold instead of the armor
To refuse, or at least demand Indra's infallible dart in return
questions.explanation: Surya urged Karna not to give up the armor, but seeing his son immovable, told him at least to demand the Shakti in exchange.

questions.q: Why did Karna give away the armor despite knowing the beggar's true identity?
options: He held his fame and vow of giving above his life
He believed Surya would protect him anyway
He wished to shame the king of the gods
questions.explanation: Karna told Surya that fame outlasts the body, and he would not stain his vow of charity even to save his life.

questions.q: At what moment of the day was Karna bound to refuse no supplicant?
options: At midday, after his worship of the sun
On the night of the full moon
At the gates of his palace each evening
questions.explanation: After his midday prayers to the sun, Karna would refuse nothing to anyone who asked, and Indra timed his visit accordingly.

questions.q: What protection had the inborn armor given Karna?
options: It made him unslayable in battle
questions.explanation: While Karna wore the natural armor and earrings, no weapon could kill him in battle.

questions.q: For whom did Karna hoard the dart he received from Indra?
questions.explanation: Karna saved the one-use Vasavi Shakti for Arjuna alone, intending it to decide their long rivalry.

questions.q: On whom was the Vasavi Shakti finally used?
questions.explanation: Ghatotkacha's night rampage grew so deadly that Karna was forced to hurl the dart at him instead of Arjuna.

questions.q: When did Karna finally hurl the one-use dart?
options: On the fourteenth night of the war
On the tenth day at dusk
On the first day of battle
questions.explanation: On the fourteenth night, with the Kaurava army collapsing under Ghatotkacha's illusions, Karna spent the weapon he had guarded for years.

questions.q: How did Krishna react when Karna's dart was finally spent?
options: He blew the Panchajanya in triumph
He danced with joy, for Arjuna was now safe
He vowed to end the war the next day
questions.explanation: Krishna rejoiced openly, for with the Shakti gone, the one weapon certain to kill Arjuna had left the world.

questions.q: What happened to the Vasavi Shakti after it killed its single victim?
questions.explanation: True to its condition, the dart slew Ghatotkacha and then flew back to Indra, leaving Karna without it.

questions.q: Which name of Karna is tied by the epic to his cutting off the armor?
questions.explanation: The epic links the name Vaikartana both to Karna's father Vikartana, the sun, and to his cutting the armor from his body.

questions.q: During which period of the story did Karna give away his armor?
options: On the eve of the eighteenth day
questions.explanation: The exchange occurs near the end of the Vana Parva, while the Pandavas were still in their forest exile.

questions.q: Who engineered the night battle that forced Karna to spend the dart early?
questions.explanation: Krishna sent Ghatotkacha against Karna at night precisely so the dart reserved for Arjuna would be used up.

questions.q: Which sub-parva of the epic narrates the taking of Karna's earrings and armor?
questions.explanation: The episode forms the Kundala-aharana Parva, the section on the taking of the earrings, within the Vana Parva.

questions.q: From what substance did Surya say the earrings had sprung, making Karna deathless while he wore them?
questions.explanation: Surya told Karna the earrings were born of amrita, so death could not touch him while they remained on his body.

questions.q: What grace did Indra grant so that the cutting would not mar Karna?
options: His pain would pass to Indra
His body would bear no disfiguring scar
His blood would turn to flowers
His strength would double at noon
questions.explanation: Moved by the gift, Indra granted that Karna's wounds would heal leaving no scar and his beauty would remain unmarred.

questions.q: How did the heavens respond as Karna sliced off his armor?
options: A rain of ash fell on Hastinapura
Celestial drums sounded and flowers fell
questions.explanation: As Karna cut his flesh without flinching, the gods beat celestial drums and showered flowers in praise of his sacrifice.

questions.q: The dart's name, Vasavi, comes from which title?
questions.explanation: Vasavi means belonging to Vasava, an epithet of Indra, marking the dart as the god's own weapon.

questions.q: What did Karna offer the disguised brahmin first, before surrendering the armor?
options: Riches, cows, land, even his kingdom
Service as a charioteer for life
A year of his own lifespan
questions.explanation: Karna offered wealth, cattle, land, and even his kingdom, but the beggar insisted on the armor and earrings alone.

questions.q: In what form did Surya appear within Karna's warning dream?
options: As a blazing wheel of light
questions.explanation: Surya came to the sleeping Karna in the guise of a brahmin, mirroring the very disguise Indra would later use.

questions.q: How did Krishna manage Arjuna's safety while Karna still held the unused dart?
options: He hid Arjuna in the rear ranks
He had Arjuna fight only at dusk
He gave Arjuna an amulet of Indra
He kept Arjuna from a decisive duel with Karna
questions.explanation: Knowing the Shakti could not miss, Krishna steered Arjuna away from a final confrontation with Karna until the dart had been spent.
